infobox.value: ([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ləˈbɒrətəri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˈlæbrətɔːri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); colloquially **lab**) is a facility that provides controlled conditions in which [scientific](/wiki/Science "Science") or technological research, [experiments](/wiki/Experiment "Experiment"), and [measurement](/wiki/Measurement "Measurement") may be performed. Laboratories are found in a variety of settings such as schools, universities, privately owned research institutions, corporate research and testing facilities, government regulatory and forensic investigation centers, physicians' offices, clinics, hospitals, regional and national referral centers, and even occasionally personal residences.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Workplace for scientific activity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/At_Manchester_2023_2023_006.jpg/250px-At_Manchester_2023_2023_006.jpg)](/wiki/File:At_Manchester_2023_2023_006.jpg)

The [Schuster Laboratory](/wiki/Schuster_Laboratory "Schuster Laboratory"), [University of Manchester](/wiki/University_of_Manchester "University of Manchester") (a physics laboratory)

A **laboratory** ([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/ləˈbɒrətəri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˈlæbrətɔːri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); colloquially **lab**) is a facility that provides controlled conditions in which [scientific](/wiki/Science "Science") or technological research, [experiments](/wiki/Experiment "Experiment"), and [measurement](/wiki/Measurement "Measurement") may be performed. Laboratories are found in a variety of settings such as schools, universities, privately owned research institutions, corporate research and testing facilities, government regulatory and forensic investigation centers, physicians' offices, clinics, hospitals, regional and national referral centers, and even occasionally personal residences.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laboratory&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

The organisation and contents of laboratories are determined by the differing requirements of the specialists working within. A [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics") laboratory might contain a [particle accelerator](/wiki/Particle_accelerator "Particle accelerator") or [vacuum chamber](/wiki/Vacuum_chamber "Vacuum chamber"), while a [metallurgy](/wiki/Metallurgy "Metallurgy") laboratory could have apparatus for [casting](/wiki/Casting_(metalworking) "Casting (metalworking)") or [refining](/wiki/Refining "Refining") metals or for testing their [strength](/wiki/Strength_of_materials "Strength of materials"). A [chemist](/wiki/Chemist "Chemist") or [biologist](/wiki/Biologist "Biologist") might use a [wet laboratory](/wiki/Wet_laboratory "Wet laboratory"), while a [psychologist's](/wiki/Psychology "Psychology") laboratory might be a room with one-way mirrors and hidden cameras in which to observe behavior. In some laboratories, such as those commonly used by [computer scientists](/wiki/Computer_scientist "Computer scientist"), [computers](/wiki/Computer "Computer") (sometimes [supercomputers](/wiki/Supercomputer "Supercomputer")) are used for either [simulations](/wiki/Computer_simulation "Computer simulation") or the [analysis](/wiki/Data_analysis "Data analysis") of [data](/wiki/Data "Data"). Scientists in other fields will still use other types of laboratories. [Engineers](/wiki/Engineers "Engineers") use laboratories as well to design, build, and test technological devices.

Scientific laboratories can be found as research room and [learning spaces](/wiki/Learning_space "Learning space") in [schools](/wiki/School "School") and [universities](/wiki/University "University"), [industry](/wiki/Private_industry "Private industry"), [government](/wiki/Government "Government"), or [military](/wiki/Military "Military") facilities, and even aboard [ships](/wiki/Ship "Ship") and [spacecraft](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/GIRLS%27_COUMTY_SCHOOL_LAB.jpg/250px-GIRLS%27_COUMTY_SCHOOL_LAB.jpg)](/wiki/File:GIRLS%27_COUMTY_SCHOOL_LAB.jpg)

Laboratory, Brecon County School for Girls

Despite the underlying notion of the lab as a confined space for experts,[[2]](#cite_note-2) the term "laboratory" is also increasingly applied to workshop spaces such as [Living Labs](/wiki/Living_lab "Living lab"), [Fab Labs](/wiki/Fab_lab "Fab lab"), or [Hackerspaces](/wiki/Hackerspace "Hackerspace"), in which people meet to work on societal problems or make [prototypes](/wiki/Prototype "Prototype"), working collaboratively or sharing resources.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) This development is inspired by new, [participatory approaches](/wiki/Participatory_science "Participatory science") to science and innovation and relies on user-centred design methods[[6]](#cite_note-6) and concepts like [Open innovation](/wiki/Open_innovation "Open innovation") or [User innovation](/wiki/User_innovation "User innovation"),.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) One distinctive feature of work in Open Labs is the phenomenon of [translation](/wiki/Translational_research "Translational research"), driven by the different backgrounds and levels of expertise of the people involved.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laboratory&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Early instances of "laboratories" recorded in English involved [alchemy](/wiki/Alchemy "Alchemy") and the preparation of [medicines](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_drug "Pharmaceutical drug").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The emergence of [Big Science](/wiki/Big_Science "Big Science") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") increased the size of laboratories and scientific equipment, introducing [particle accelerators](/wiki/Particle_accelerator "Particle accelerator") and similar devices.

### Early laboratories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laboratory&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early laboratories")]

The earliest laboratory according to the present evidence is a home laboratory of [Pythagoras of Samos](/wiki/Pythagoras_of_Samos "Pythagoras of Samos"). This laboratory was created when Pythagoras conducted an experiment about tones of sound and vibration of string.[[11]](#cite_note-11) A 16th century underground alchemical laboratory, known as Speculum Alcemiae, was accidentally discovered in the year 2002. [Rudolf II, Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Rudolf_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Rudolf II, Holy Roman Emperor") was believed to be the owner. The laboratory is preserved as a museum in [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In the 1885 painting of [Louis Pasteur](/wiki/Louis_Pasteur "Louis Pasteur") by [Albert Edelfelt](/wiki/Albert_Edelfelt "Albert Edelfelt"), Pasteur is shown comparing a note in his left hand with a bottle filled with a solid in his right hand, and not wearing any [personal protective equipment](/wiki/Personal_protective_equipment "Personal protective equipment").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Researching in teams started in the 19th century, and many new kinds of equipment were developed in the 20th century.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laboratory&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Techniques")]

Laboratory techniques are the set of procedures used on [natural sciences](/wiki/Natural_sciences "Natural sciences") such as [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), [biology](/wiki/Biology "Biology"), [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics") to conduct an experiment; while some of them involve the use of complex laboratory equipment from [laboratory glassware](/wiki/Laboratory_glassware "Laboratory glassware") to electrical devices, and others require more specific or expensive supplies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Equipment and supplies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laboratory&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Equipment and supplies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Lab_glassware.jpg/250px-Lab_glassware.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lab_glassware.jpg)

Three [beakers](/wiki/Beaker_(glassware) "Beaker (glassware)"), an [Erlenmeyer flask](/wiki/Erlenmeyer_flask "Erlenmeyer flask"), a [graduated cylinder](/wiki/Graduated_cylinder "Graduated cylinder") and a [volumetric flask](/wiki/Volumetric_flask "Volumetric flask")

Laboratory equipment refers to the various tools and equipment used by scientists working in a laboratory. Laboratory equipment is generally used to either perform an [experiment](/wiki/Experiment "Experiment") or to take [measurements](/wiki/Measurement "Measurement") and gather [data](/wiki/Data "Data"). Larger or more sophisticated equipment is generally called a [scientific instrument](/wiki/Scientific_instrument "Scientific instrument").

The classical equipment includes tools such as [Bunsen burners](/wiki/Bunsen_burner "Bunsen burner") and [microscopes](/wiki/Microscope "Microscope") as well as specialty equipment such as [operant conditioning chambers](/wiki/Operant_conditioning_chamber "Operant conditioning chamber"), [bioreactors](/wiki/Bioreactor "Bioreactor"), [hematology analyzers](/wiki/Hematology_analyzer "Hematology analyzer"), [autoclaves](/wiki/Autoclave "Autoclave"), [centrifuges](/wiki/Centrifuge "Centrifuge"), [spectrophotometers](/wiki/Spectrophotometer "Spectrophotometer") and [calorimeters](/wiki/Calorimeter "Calorimeter"), [glucometer](/wiki/Glucose_meter "Glucose meter"), [incubator](/wiki/Incubator_(culture) "Incubator (culture)").

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laboratory&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Specialized types")]

The title of *laboratory* is also used for other facilities where the processes or equipment used are similar to those in scientific laboratories. These include:

* [Film laboratories](/wiki/Film_laboratory "Film laboratory") or [darkrooms](/wiki/Darkroom "Darkroom")
* [Clandestine labs](/wiki/Clandestine_chemistry "Clandestine chemistry") for the production of [illegal drugs](/wiki/Illegal_drug "Illegal drug")
* [Computer labs](/wiki/Computer_lab "Computer lab")

## Safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laboratory&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Safety")]

Main article: [Laboratory safety](/wiki/Laboratory_safety "Laboratory safety")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/2008-07-02_Eye_wash_station.jpg/250px-2008-07-02_Eye_wash_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:2008-07-02_Eye_wash_station.jpg)

An [eyewash](/wiki/Eyewash "Eyewash") station in a laboratory

In many laboratories, hazards are present. Laboratory hazards might include [poisons](/wiki/Poison "Poison"); [infectious agents](/wiki/Infectious_agent "Infectious agent"); [flammable](/wiki/Flammable "Flammable"), [explosive](/wiki/Explosive "Explosive"), or [radioactive](/wiki/Radioactive "Radioactive") materials; moving [machinery](/wiki/Machinery "Machinery"); extreme [temperatures](/wiki/Temperature "Temperature"); [lasers](/wiki/Laser "Laser"), strong [magnetic fields](/wiki/Magnetic_field "Magnetic field") or [high voltage](/wiki/High_voltage "High voltage"). Therefore, [safety](/wiki/Safety "Safety") precautions are vitally important.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) Rules exist to minimize the individual's risk, and [safety equipment](/wiki/Safety_equipment "Safety equipment") is used to protect lab users from injury or to assist in responding to an [emergency](/wiki/Emergency "Emergency").

The [Occupational Safety and Health Administration](/wiki/Occupational_Safety_and_Health_Administration "Occupational Safety and Health Administration") (OSHA) in the United States, recognizing the unique characteristics of the laboratory workplace, has tailored a standard for occupational exposure to hazardous chemicals in laboratories. This standard is often referred to as the "Laboratory Standard". Under this standard, a laboratory is required to produce a Chemical Hygiene Plan (CHP) which addresses the specific hazards found in its location, and its approach to them.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In determining the proper Chemical Hygiene Plan for a particular business or laboratory, it is necessary to understand the requirements of the standard, evaluation of the current safety, health and environmental practices and assessment of the hazards. Many schools and businesses employ safety, health, and environmental specialists, such as a Chemical Hygiene Officer to develop, manage, and evaluate their CHP. Additionally, third party review is also used to provide an objective "outside view" which provides a fresh look at areas and problems that may be taken for granted or overlooked due to habit.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Inspections and audits like also be conducted on a regular basis to assess hazards due to chemical handling and storage, electrical equipment, [biohazards](/wiki/Biohazards "Biohazards"), [hazardous waste management](/wiki/Hazardous_waste_management "Hazardous waste management"), [chemical waste](/wiki/Chemical_waste#Laboratory_waste_containment "Chemical waste"), housekeeping and emergency preparedness, [radiation safety](/wiki/Radiation_safety "Radiation safety"), ventilation as well as respiratory testing and [indoor air quality](/wiki/Indoor_air_quality "Indoor air quality"). An important element of such audits is the review of regulatory compliance and the training of individuals who have access to or work in the laboratory. Training is critical to the ongoing safe operation of the laboratory facility. Educators, staff and management must be engaged in working to reduce the likelihood of accidents, injuries and potential litigation. Efforts are often made to ensure laboratory safety videos are both relevant and engaging.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Sustainability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laboratory&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sustainability")]

The effects of [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") are becoming more of a concern for organizations, and [mitigation strategies](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation") are being sought by the research community. While many laboratories are used to perform research to find innovative solutions to this global challenge, sustainable working practices in the labs are also contributing factors towards a greener environment. Many labs, such as those at the [Massacusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology")[[18]](#cite_note-18) and the [University of Edinburgh](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh "University of Edinburgh"),[[19]](#cite_note-19) are already trying to minimize their [environmental impact](/wiki/Environmental_impact "Environmental impact") by reducing energy consumption, recycling, and implementing waste sorting processes to ensure correct disposal.

Research labs featuring energy-intensive equipment use up to three to five times more energy per square meter than office areas. Major contributors to this high energy consumption are [fume hoods](/wiki/Fume_hood "Fume hood").[[20]](#cite_note-:1-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Fume hoods put a significant load on a buildings heating and cooling systems, as they remove high volumes of conditioned air from a lab when in use.[[22]](#cite_note-:6-22) Sensors,[[23]](#cite_note-:17-23) automatic shutoff systems,[[24]](#cite_note-:7-24) and awareness campaigns to close the sash window on fume hoods have been used to decrease the energy consumption of these devices.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Normally, [ultra-low temperature freezers](/wiki/ULT_freezer "ULT freezer") are kept at −80 °C (−112 °F). One such device can consume up to the same amount of energy as a single-family household does in a day (25 kWh).[[26]](#cite_note-26) Increasing the temperature to −70 °C (−94 °F) makes it possible to use 40% less energy and still keep most samples safely stored.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Minimizing the consumption of water can be achieved by changing from water-cooled condensers ([*Dimroth* condenser](/wiki/Condenser_(laboratory) "Condenser (laboratory)")) to air-cooled condensers ([*Vigreux* column](/wiki/Condenser_(laboratory) "Condenser (laboratory)")), which take advantage of a larger surface area to cool. Ovens used to dry glassware can consume a lot of energy. Employing timers to regulate their use during nights and weekends can reduce their impact on energy consumption enormously.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The disposal of chemically/biologically contaminated waste requires a lot of energy. Regular waste requires much less energy and in some cases can even be [recycled](/wiki/Recycling "Recycling"). Not every object in a lab is contaminated, but often ends up in the contaminated waste, driving energy costs for waste disposal. A good sorting and recycling system for non-contaminated lab waste can allow lab users to act sustainably and correctly dispose of waste.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laboratory&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Organization")]

Organization of laboratories is an area of focus in sociology. Scientists consider how their work should be organized, which could be based on themes, teams, projects or fields of expertise. Work is divided, not only between different jobs of the laboratory such as the researchers, engineers and [technicians](/wiki/Laboratory_technician "Laboratory technician"), but also in terms of autonomy (should the work be individual or in groups).[[29]](#cite_note-29) For example, one research group has a schedule where they conduct research on their own topic of interest for one day of the week, but for the rest they work on a given group project.[[30]](#cite_note-:0-30) Finance management is yet another organizational issue.

The laboratory itself is a historically dated organizational model. It came about due to the observation that the quality of work of researchers who collaborate is overall greater than a researcher working in isolation. From the 1950s, the laboratory has evolved from being an educational tool used by teachers to attract the top students into research, into an organizational model allowing a high level of scientific productivity.

Some forms of organization in laboratories include:

* **Their size**: Varies from a handful of researches to several hundred.
* **The division of labor**: Work divided among those working in the laboratory and all other professions involved in the course of the research, ranging from approving committees to designers, technicians, and researchers.[[31]](#cite_note-31)
* **The coordination mechanisms**: Which includes the formalization of objectives and tasks; the standardization of procedures (protocols, project management, quality management, knowledge management), the validation of publications and cross-cutting activities (number and type of seminars).

There are three main factors that contribute to the organizational form of a laboratory :

* The educational background of the researchers and their socialization process.
* The intellectual process involved in their work, including the type of investigation and equipment they use.
* The laboratory's history.

Other forms of organization include social organization.

### Social organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Laboratory&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Social organization")]

A study by Richard H.R. Harper, involving two laboratories, will help elucidate the concept of social organization in laboratories. The main subject of the study revolved around the relationship between the staff of a laboratory (researchers, administrators, receptionists, technicians, etc.) and their Locator. A Locator is an employee of a Laboratory who is in charge of knowing where each member of the laboratory currently is, based on a unique signal emitted from the badge of each staff member. The study describes social relationships among different classes of jobs, such as the relationship between researchers and the Locator. It does not describe the social relationship between employees within a class, such as the relationship between researchers.

Through [ethnographic](/wiki/Ethnography "Ethnography") studies, one finding is that, among the personnel, each class (researchers, administrators...) has a different degree of entitlement, which varies per laboratory. Entitlement can be both formal or informal (meaning it is not enforced), but each class is aware and conforms to its existence. The degree of entitlement, which is also referred to as a staff's [rights](/wiki/Rights "Rights"), affects social interaction between staff. By looking at the various interactions among staff members, we can determine their social position in the organization. As an example, administrators, in one lab of the study, do not have the right to ask the Locator where the researchers currently are, as they are not entitled to such information. On the other hand, researchers do have access to this type of information. So a consequence of this social hierarchy is that the Locator discloses various degrees of information, based on the staff member and their rights. The Locator does not want to disclose information that could jeopardize his relationship with the members of staff. The Locator adheres to the rights of each class.

Social hierarchy is also related to attitudes towards technologies. This was inferred based on the attitude of various jobs towards their lab badge. Their attitude depended on how that job viewed their badge from a standpoint of utility, (how is the badge useful for my job) morality (what are my morals on privacy, as it relates to being tracked by this badge) and relations (how will I be seen by others if I refuse to wear this badge). For example, a receptionist would view the badge as useful, as it would help them locate members of staff during the day. Illustrating relations, researchers would also wear their badge due to informal pressures, such as not wanting to look like a spoil-sport, or not wanting to draw attention to themselves.

Another finding is the resistance to change in a social organization. Staff members feel ill at ease when changing patterns of entitlement, obligation, respect, informal and formal hierarchy, and more.

In summary, differences in attitude among members of the laboratory are explained by social organization: A person's attitudes are intimately related to the role they have in an organization. This hierarchy helps understand information distribution, control, and attitudes towards technologies in the laboratory.[[30]](#cite_note-:0-30)
